Using a new, top-down framework that combines national lending conditions with market-level valuation dynamics, we find that refinancing stress is driven by a relatively small subset of markets, property types, and vintages. Office faces the most visible challenges, but these are concentrated in a handful of gateway markets. Industrial remains broadly resilient, retail sits in a middle ground with market specific risks, and apartments, while showing strong headline refinancing capacity, exhibit thinning cushions in later vintages and select markets.

Overall, refinancing risk is real but uneven, with outcomes hinging on location, asset quality, and vintage rather than a uniform downturn across the CRE market. We believe this will ultimately result in the outcome for the wall of maturities being far better than feared.

Appendix: Methodology

Our analysis combines lending market data at the national level from Real Capital Analytics (RCA) with local property market data from CoStar to estimate refinancing outcomes across origination vintages, property types, and geographies. The objective is to assess refinancing feasibility at a market-level under prevailing conditions, rather than to evaluate outcomes for individual loans or assets.

RCA metrics:

  • Mortgage rates
  • Loan-to-value (LTV) standards
  • Debt service coverage ratio (DSCR) requirements

CoStar metrics:

  • Asset values **1
  • Net operating income (NOI) growth
  • Market cap rates

First, we estimate changes in lending conditions using RCA data for mortgage rates, loan-to-value standards, and debt service coverage ratios for both core commercial and apartment properties. These metrics are used to approximate the then-current underwriting standards on a quarterly basis for the six-year period from 2016 to 2021 and compare them to current standards. For instance, RCA estimates that LTVs stood at 65% in Q1 2016 compared to 58% today, and DSCR stood at 1.57x compared to 1.68x today. This allows us to isolate the impact of tighter lending conditions on potential capital gaps.

EXHIBIT 12: Debt metrics

**1 CoStar asset values reflect modeled estimates of current market value derived from a transaction-based, comparable-sales framework. For assets that have not transacted recently, prices are inferred using comparable sales and adjusted for location, size, age, rent levels, and other observable property characteristics. Modeled values are trended forward using same-store rent and occupancy data to capture changes in local income fundamentals, and further adjusted to reflect broad capital market conditions through national cap rate trends.

Next, we use CoStar data to estimate origination-period NOI by using market-level asset values and cap rates. To ensure that changes in inventory are not driving the changes in NOI, we express these inputs on a per-square-foot basis (or per-unit for multifamily), effectively normalizing market-level values before applying growth assumptions. We then apply cumulative NOI growth over the assumed term of the loan to generate an NOI value as of 2026 and capitalize that income using current cap rates to approximate an implied refinance-period asset value for each property type across all markets.

For example, in Washington, DC, for industrial, the NOI index increases by roughly 55% from Q1 2021 to Q1 2026. This more than offsets the modest rise in cap rates from 6.28% to 6.75% over the same time period. As a result, the modeled refinance outcome produces a capital surplus of 17.9% of value, implying excess borrowing capacity even as LTVs modestly tightened from 58.1% to 57.4%.

EXHIBIT 13: Annual NOI growth and cap rates

This implied value forms the basis for evaluating refinance proceeds relative to current lending standards. Specifically, we calculate the maximum refinance loan amount constrained by both loan-to-value limits and debt service coverage requirements, with the more restrictive of the two determining refinance capacity.

Finally, the estimated loan is compared to the origination loan, generating a capital gap (or surplus) for each property type across markets for a given period. We analyze cap gaps (or surpluses) across quartiles in what's known as a box-and-whiskers (box plot) analysis to visualize the range of potential outcomes across markets. Outliers on the box-and-whiskers charts are identified using a standard methodology designed to highlight observations that differ materially from the typical range of outcomes. For each sector-vintage distribution, results are summarized by the middle 50% of observations that are bounded by the 25th percentile (lower quartile) and 75th percentile (upper quartile). The difference between these two values is referred to as the interquartile range (IQR). Observations that fall more than 1.5 times this range below the lower quartile or above the upper quartile are classified as outliers and displayed separately. This approach highlights markets with outcomes that are meaningfully distinct from their peers without imposing arbitrary thresholds.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Agree Realty: Reliable Dividend Income To Weather The Macro Storm
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Agree Realty (ADC), I called them a “High-Quality Monthly Dividend REIT In An Uncertain World,” pointing out their high-quality portfolio, attractive yield, and solid growth potential despite near-term macro headwinds.

Although the Iran conflict is still not over and we can see significantly higher levels of risk and uncertainty that should demand a higher margin of safety even from a high-quality company like this, ADC already seems to trade at a level that implies it, with the company being in a very strong position and still having room to improve even in a weaker environment.

Starting 2026 on a Strong Note

ADC reported a strong Q1, beating the market’s revenue and FFO estimates while maintaining their industry-leading investment-grade tenant concentration (65%) and raising ~$660 million through their forward equity ATM program, all while the AFFO per share increased by a very strong 7.9% to $1.14.

ADC also reaffirmed its $4.54 to $4.58 AFFO per share guidance (up 5.4% YoY at midpoint) despite a $0.02 to $0.04 dilutive impact from their stock issuance program, still expecting a massive $1.4 billion to $1.6 billion investment volume on only $25 million to $75 million worth of dispositions, while pointing out the following regarding their near-term acquisition pipeline during their Q1 Earnings Call:

So our pipeline, as I mentioned in the prepared remarks, across all 3 platforms is very strong. There are 2 things that will determine, frankly, our pace into Q2. Number one is just the macro environment here. Obviously, we have a significant amount of uncertainty that seems to change by the hour. And then two, at our election, which transactions we decide to pursue. So we have a number of transactions across all 3 platforms that are under contract or under letter of intent going through the diligence period. But all 3 pipelines are extremely strong.

Financially, based on ADC’s latest report, we continue to see a strong position, backed by rising assets as part of their investing activity, while the equity is also growing since they’re using their stock to expand, maintaining a very attractive pro forma net debt to recurring EBITDA ratio of 3.2x when including the settlement of their outstanding forward equity (5.1x without it).

ADC also increased the monthly dividend to $0.267, for a yield of about 4.15% per year and about $385.68 million, which represents a very solid payout ratio of about 70.26% based on the midpoint of their 2026 AFFO guidance, leaving plenty of room for increases if they want to, although that would also affect their ATM program since the cost (yield) would be higher.

There’s also the company’s preferred stock (ADC.PR.A) paying a very attractive 6.21% yield at the moment of writing this, with a $25 liquidation preference compared to a price of $17.10, becoming redeemable on September 17, 2026, and being well covered by a high-quality company’s income with solid credit ratings.

However, since it comes with a 4.25% base cost for them, I doubt the company would want to redeem it since it’s very close to their current costs of capital (similar to the 4.15% dividend yield on the stock and the 4.02% on the recent $350 million 5.5-year term loan), and even if they decide to call them when that’s possible, I don’t think a ~46.2% profit plus the accumulated dividends is bad for less than 5 months of holding.

Post-Conflict Risks Rising

At this point, the Iran conflict has added another month since the previous article, which elevates the risk significantly. Not only are commodity prices still elevated, but the effects can last longer given the damage done to the region’s energy infrastructure, potentially resulting in an environment where war premiums persist, as we could see weak supply (since flows can take months or even years to recover) combined with a boost in demand from countries or companies wanting to replenish their reserves quickly.

For example, the EIA increased its near-term oil outlook recently, expecting oil prices to reach an average of $88/b in Q4 2026 and remain elevated through 2027, where they expect Brent at an average of $76/b, well above the low $60s (or even below) seen at the beginning of 2026, which is about $23/b higher than their February forecast.

This can translate into pressure for the economy for longer than the market is currently accounting for, potentially leading to an inflationary wave that would force the Fed and the other central banks to rethink their rate policies to combat it. Again, this conflict is crucial for many commodities that have deep ramifications in the global economy, and if we don’t see an end soon followed by a rapid recovery, we could see worse and worse outcomes, with the IMF releasing a similar warning not long ago.

Meanwhile, Warsh (or whoever will get all the approvals) will likely have a very rough start to their term as Fed Chair, taking the helm during what the IEA Chief recently described as an “extremely fragile situation,” warning that the Hormuz crisis can be the biggest energy security threat in history.

It’s also important to note that the Fed Chair is not the sole person responsible for the country’s rate decisions, as they are still just 1 vote out of 12 in the FOMC, so even if we were to see a person who does what the President wants, the others can still act as a safety cushion for independence in these decisions.

This is something that goes well beyond ADC’s borrowing costs, affecting their clients’ financial health, the consumer’s disposable income, and even the attractiveness of ADC’s stock, as high treasury yields can change the risk-adjusted reward the market demands from it in the near term, leading to weaker valuations to price in the new risks.

On the other hand, such a negative environment can further pressure the new supply in the country’s real estate, potentially allowing ADC to benefit from its high-quality portfolio and get better rates on their leasing and investing activity, further strengthening a potential recovery when the inflationary shock is over and rate cuts continue.

This can be a solid catalyst for a potential recovery in the industry, as we saw the new supply fall or normalize in many segments of real estate following the post-pandemic boom that’s being absorbed, combined with soft demand and higher costs/interest rates that slowed down new projects.

Risks Reiterated

Even though we’re talking about a company with high-quality properties and great management, they are still limited by the environment they operate in, and there’s certainly the possibility to see the Iran conflict result in weakness for longer.

This can result in the company’s valuation being affected regardless of how they perform, as the market can adjust the valuation of this asset class in a higher-for-longer interest environment (as seen over the past few years), which is normal.

Valuation

For ADC’s DCF to Equity, updating the starting AFFO to $548.91 million based on the company’s guidance and current number of shares, I’ll continue to assume a 5% CAGR until 2030 thanks to their massive acquisition pipeline, their rent escalators, and broader economic improvements, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, but will go for a 9.5% discount rate now to assume a better margin of safety, with 120.375 million shares outstanding as of their Q1 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $9.07 billion, for an intrinsic value of $75.36 per share, slightly below the current $77.25 at the moment of writing this, implying that the market is still pricing in a solid level of risk.

Conclusions

ADC and ADC.PR.A remain rated Buy, backed by a strong and healthy company with a massive growth pipeline and great management navigating the tough environment well.

As highlighted during the previous analysis as well, the current valuation is fair if we imply a >9% discount rate, which can be enough to justify the Buy rating even in the current environment despite the potential to see macro weakness that can come after the conflict is over lead to better prices.

data.title: Cummins: Earnings Beat On The Way
data.description:
Cummins (CMI) is set to report earnings in a couple of weeks, and it's a good time to dust off the model and focus on what's ahead. The diesel engine maker has set forth an impressive guidance slate for the year ahead, looking for growth in all major segments, along with a kicker in Power demand stemming from the appetite for data centers.

Business Profile

Cummins has 5 distinct segments:

  • Engine - the company manufactures primarily diesel engines that go into everything from trucks, and buses, to construction and mining
  • Components - this segment largely revolves around parts that go into the engines themselves, so they own the value chain; it includes parts like turbochargers, fuel systems, emissions systems, and filtration
  • Distribution - repair, systems, and maintenance for the parts and engines Cummins produces; the company operates a global dealer and service network
  • Power - the company builds power systems like generators, industrial and marine engines, and power solutions for large infrastructure
  • Accelera - this is the energy transition segment focused on next-generation non-diesel engine technology like hydrogen fuel cells, electrolyzers, and battery-electric powertrains

All of these segments have their own guidance, seen below:

Source: Investor Presentation

To better understand the breakdown here, Engine makes up 26% of sales, Components 25%, Distribution 30% and Power 18%. The balance is Accelera at just 1%. There have been small shifts under the hood, but things like Power Systems have increased in penetration (+2 points in 2025 versus 2024) because of demand from things like data centers. To no surprise, that is why it's the fastest-growing segment at CMI.

The company serves a wide variety of end markets in its power division. Everything from data centers to defense to public transportation, the company is involved. This makes it susceptible to global GDP growth as well as global industrial growth.

Financial Profile and Forward Estimates

Recall the guide:

  • Revenue +3-8%
  • Earnings from JVs: -5%/+5%
  • EBITDA margin 17-18%
  • D&A $1.1-$1.2 billion
  • Tax rate 24%
  • Capex $1.35-$1.45 billion
  • Global Pension Funding: $51 million
  • Interest Expense $350 million

To start to build the quarter ahead of earnings, let's focus on what happened last year. Both Engine and Components lagged last year, down 5% and 20%, respectively, in the 2025 Q1. Now, the Components separation was exacerbated by the Separation of Altmus, but there was still weakness in on-highway demand in the US and Europe. With that being said, the Distribution and Power Systems segments both were quite strong and will serve as tough y/y compares. Distribution was +22% in North America for a +15% total segment gain, while Power Systems was up 15% in North America with a total segment gain of 19%.

Total revenue was down 3% last year, so total CMI has somewhat of an easy comparison. If we extrapolate the midpoint of annual guidance to Q1 sales, we arrive at +5.5% sales growth y/y to $8.62 billion. This compares to the street estimate of $8.35 billion.

I am assuming a 50 bps expansion on gross margin to 26.9%. I am assuming no material leverage on SG&A and flat R&D as a percentage of sales. Equity income as well as other expenses tends to be relatively stable, and I am not altering them as a percentage of sales. This shakes out to $1.24 billion in operating income, up about 9% y/y for a great result.

Using a 24% tax rate and interest expense per quarter of $87.5 million (annual guide: $350 million), we arrive at $6.62 in EPS. Street is looking for EPS of $5.62, so what I've modeled is a very clear beat, largely flowing through a meaningful revenue delta, leverage on the gross margin line, and stable other income/equity royalty components. These estimates look beatable, which bodes well for CMI.

The material earnings beat also makes for an interesting setup into the company's analyst day on May 21. Management will likely unveil new multi-year targets. It hasn't been too long since the last analyst day, which occurred in 2024, but they have a pattern of doing an analyst day every 2 years. The targets are set for 2030, and I imagine they will not go past 2030, as there's still five fiscal years within that. Recall the last algorithm:

  • Base business: $39-$42 billion
    • Operating cash flow > $35 billion from 2022-2030
    • 25% incremental EBITDA growth
  • Accelera $3-$9 billion in revenue in 2030
    • $1.5-$2 bn cash outflow from operations 2022-2027
    • Breakeven in 2027
  • Total Cummins
    • $43-$48 billion
    • Operating cash flow: $34-$36 billion
    • 17-18% EBITDA margin in 2030

The company noted that the base business excluded Altmus. This is due to the fact that the company separated from Altmus in March of 2024.

I thought it was helpful that the last time the company had its analyst day, they gave the margin bridge. This is so it's clear how the progress would actually transpire:

Source: Investor Presentation

With an Analyst Day just around the corner, the earnings beat could create positive momentum into that day and could lead to better targets through the end of the decade. A higher margin target would be the most positive development for the stock.

Valuation

It should be noted that we can use P/E to evaluate Cummins cleanly versus peers. The company is not levered materially and has leverage of just 0.5x on a net basis.

I am using the peer set of ALSN, ETN, PH, and DAN. While all of these companies do slightly different things, they are diversified industrial companies and have a similar operating earnings profile to that of CMI.

  • Dana Incorporated (DAN) - trades at a forward P/E of 14.3x
  • Allison Transmission (ALSN) - trades at a forward P/E of 14.8x
  • Eaton (ETN) - trades at a forward P/E of 32x
  • Parker-Hannifin (PH) - trades at a forward valuation of 31.4x

Now, this is a very wide multiple gap among diversified industrial names and I think it's worth mentioning that the discount between the likes of DAN/ALSN versus that of PH and ETN is primarily due to the heavy exposure to capex cycle cyclicality. Being more dependent on the macroeconomic environment translates to a discounted valuation.

With that being said, Cummins currently trades at 24.9x forward earnings, and I see little reason why it can't close the gap with Eaton or Parker, especially given the strength in Power and Distribution. I would not argue for a full gap closure to Parker since the company sports substantially higher margins. Segment operating income margins alone are in the mid-20s versus roughly 14% for Cummins. The case is the same for Eaton. This places more emphasis on how Cummins moves the range on the EBITDA margin target at the upcoming Analyst day.

Everything seems to be going the right way for Cummins but the stock chart, at first glance, can be rather intimidating given the positive performance. It is already up 26% YTD and has had a large snap-back as the geopolitical headlines have become less volatile. Despite this chart, it can still have several turns of upside, especially if there's scope for an increased margin target at the upcoming Analyst Day. This is a high-multiple stock with a lot of momentum, so it is going to be susceptible to momentum factor drawdowns should the market experience that in the near-term.

I'm looking for a 28x P/E multiple against $28.50 in earnings for an $800 stock.

Source: Passage Research

Risks

There are multiple risks for investing in Cummins. First, given that Cummins makes primarily diesel engines, there is a risk that they do not gain equivalent market share in the forthcoming energy transition away from diesel and towards electrification, as well as alternative engine types. With diesel engines especially, there is a risk of emissions regulation and tightened standards by the government that could adversely affect the company.

The primary risk, however, is really the cyclicality of the heavy truck industry. The industry can swing materially, and given that Cummins is an OEM for engines, it maintains heavy capex cycle risk. While the company has a sizable exposure to the aftermarket, which is much more stable, there is still a clear risk of economic cyclicality. Gauging this through PMI changes is the best approach.

Conclusion

For Cummins, the story of high demand in global power generation, as well as pickup trucks, will likely continue to be the case for the foreseeable future, which helps to offset the weakness in the heavy truck market, particularly in North America. Street estimates look far too low for Q1, which sets up a beat and raise path for the company this year. Heading into an analyst day immediately after Q1 earnings means a higher EBITDA margin target could come through and boost shares. The stock, even after the run it has had, is still a buy.
